solvents.name: Water
guide_notes: CHEM21: preferred solvent class where chemically feasible.

solvents.name: Ethanol
guide_notes: CHEM21/Pfizer/GSK-style guides generally rate ethanol as preferred or usable.

solvents.name: Ethyl acetate
guide_notes: Commonly listed as a preferred ester solvent in solvent selection guides.

solvents.name: Acetone
guide_notes: Commonly usable, but low boiling point and high flammability can be limiting.

solvents.name: 2-MeTHF
guide_notes: Often used as a greener ether alternative, with peroxide controls required.

solvents.name: CPME
guide_notes: Used as an ether solvent alternative; peroxide monitoring remains necessary.

solvents.name: Acetonitrile
guide_notes: Often flagged as problematic because of toxicity and disposal concerns.

solvents.name: Methanol
guide_notes: Generally less preferred because of acute toxicity hazards.

solvents.name: Toluene
guide_notes: Usually discouraged where greener replacements are available.

solvents.name: DMSO
guide_notes: Useful polar aprotic solvent, but high boiling point complicates removal.

solvents.name: Isopropanol
guide_notes: Commonly acceptable alcohol solvent where secondary alcohol reactivity is not problematic.

solvents.name: n-Butanol
guide_notes: Higher boiling alcohol; can be useful but removal and irritation hazards matter.

solvents.name: Heptane
guide_notes: Often preferred over hexane for some nonpolar operations, but still flammable.

solvents.name: Hexane
guide_notes: Generally discouraged; heptane or other options are often preferred.

solvents.name: Methyl tert-butyl ether
guide_notes: Ether solvent with peroxide and flammability controls required.

solvents.name: Tetrahydrofuran
guide_notes: Often discouraged where 2-MeTHF or CPME can substitute.

solvents.name: Dimethyl carbonate
guide_notes: Often considered a greener solvent/reagent candidate when compatible.

solvents.name: Propylene carbonate
guide_notes: Low volatility polar solvent; high boiling point can complicate isolation.

solvents.name: Ethylene glycol
guide_notes: High boiling polar protic solvent; toxicity and removal should be considered.

solvents.name: Dichloromethane
guide_notes: Usually discouraged because of health and environmental concerns.

solvents.name: Chloroform
guide_notes: Strongly discouraged in modern solvent selection frameworks.

solvents.name: N,N-Dimethylformamide
guide_notes: Often targeted for replacement because of reproductive toxicity concerns.

solvents.name: N-Methyl-2-pyrrolidone
guide_notes: Often discouraged because of reproductive toxicity concerns.

solvents.name: Cyclopentyl methyl ether
guide_notes: Same solvent family as CPME entry; useful alias record for parser robustness.

solvents.name: tert-Butyl acetate
guide_notes: Ester solvent option sometimes used as a greener replacement candidate.

reported_replacements.note: Solvent selection guides commonly list ethyl acetate as a preferred replacement candidate for some polar organic workups and reactions.
2-MeTHF is a guide-supported greener ether solvent candidate, subject to peroxide controls and reaction-specific validation.
Ethanol may lower acute toxicity concerns but polarity and substrate solubility must be experimentally checked.
Ethanol is commonly preferred over methanol where chemistry and solubility allow.
2-MeTHF can sometimes replace hydrocarbon or ether media, subject to compatibility checks.
Heptane is commonly preferred over n-hexane in solvent replacement discussions because it avoids n-hexane neurotoxicity concerns.
2-MeTHF is widely discussed as a greener THF replacement where peroxide and process controls are acceptable.
Ethyl acetate may replace DCM in some extraction or chromatography contexts, but solvent strength and compatibility must be checked.
Propylene carbonate is a lower-volatility polar solvent candidate for some DMF replacement studies.
Dimethyl carbonate is a greener candidate but polarity and substrate solubility can differ strongly from NMP.
Ethyl acetate may be considered for some workup/extraction substitutions, with process-specific validation required.
Isopropanol can reduce acute toxicity concerns relative to methanol, but reactivity and solubility may change.
Dimethyl carbonate is sometimes explored as a greener polar aprotic-like replacement candidate.
